visual_notes: The labradorescence appears as a floating, depth-inhabiting iridescence that shifts with viewing angle and light direction. Unlike surface iridescence, it appears to come from within the stone. The dark gray to near-black base color intensifies the color display. Best specimens show full-spectrum color shift across a single face.
prompt_fragments: floating prismatic aurora iridescence in dark gray feldspar
northern lights imprisoned in polished stone
vibe_notes: Labradorite carries its color inside — it is not a surface phenomenon but a depth effect. The dark ground color against the shifting iridescence creates a sense of something luminous moving beneath a dark surface. Spectrolite, with its near-black matrix and full-spectrum display, is one of the most visually complex single mineral specimens on earth.
folklore_summary: Inuit legend describes the aurora borealis as frozen light trapped inside labradorite. Moravian missionaries encountering the stone in Labrador in 1770 were reportedly struck by the play of color.
